rationale: This is a classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction. Tert-butanol is protonated by sulfuric acid and loses water to form the stable tert-butyl carbocation. This carbocation is then trapped by the nucleophilic nitrogen of acetonitrile, forming a nitrilium ion intermediate. Subsequent hydrolysis yields N-tert-butylacetamide. This example clearly illustrates the key transformation: the formation of a C-N bond between a carbocation source and a nitrile, followed by hydrolysis to an N-substituted amide.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic, saturated reactants (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with other named reactions. It avoids arenes (Friedel-Crafts), complex functional groups, or specific ring systems that might trigger other named reaction queries. The core transformation is clearly the addition of a nitrile to a carbocation, which is the defining feature of the Ritter reaction.
